sentences.text: You'll have-- you'll have a job to stop me, this is the trouble.
I'll tell you.
Like people ask me today, how did it start?
I told you the beginning on the last tape.
It go and go.
But then I realized something.
I realized how many children-- never quite how many, but I realized we had lost children to our Jewish faith.
And they were, and are, as important to me, or even more important, than the Jewish ones who don't need us quite as much.
They've got each other, they've got their synagogues, their communities, their friends.
But what does one to find the children who got lost?
And usually the babies got lost.
The little ones.
And that is something I'll never forgive the Refugee Committee for, as long as I live, to let the little children go into non-Jewish homes.
I-- I've got a lot of non-Jewish friends.
It's not anything that's anti-non-Jew.
It isn't.
But to let little children go and lose their identities, and lose their roots, and lose the generations.
That is something I cannot forgive or forget.
Anyway, how do I find them?
I go to the BBC.
I've become quite bold.
I used to be-- I must also say-- I used to be a very quiet person.
I had a great inferiority complex, which only left me a few years ago.
A great inferiority complex.
Started at the school days, because one of my teachers told me-- I will say it in German, what she told me, that's what I'm saying.
Going back right to my school days, she said, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You will never amount to anything.
And I was 12 years old, and I believed her.
All of my life I suffered because of that sentence, because I believed it.
And that's what I meant by them being anti-Polish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] children.
Anyway, so I go to the BBC Woman's Hour.
And they are quite interested, and they will give me 10 minutes.
And that was in August, 1988.
And I put my views about the Kindertransport reunion, which I'm organizing.
By then I've already opened a-- I have my-- my cards, which I still have to this day-- index cards with names and everything, particulars.
Former names, town of origin, how old an entry, and the date of their entry.
[SIGHS] And I'm missing the ones who came, and the families if they came to Jewish or non-Jewish families.
After that broadcast, it was a revelation.
It was the most amazing thing.
The most incredible story that we've got-- the most famous of all our Kinder-- is, of course, Susi Bechhofer.
She calls herself Susi Bechhofer now.
She's married with a child.
She was deprived of her identity completely.
She came with her twin sister on her third birthday from Munich, as it happens, from the orphanage.
It was a Kinderheim.
It was a children's home, not only an orphanage, in Munich.
And she was adopted by a Baptist minister.
Her book is coming out shortly and she has got her own story to tell.
But she wrote to me.
She said, please help me.
I don't know who I am.
She had found out by then her mo-- they never told her of their identity.
They robbed them off their names.
Instead of Susi and Lotte, they were called Grace and Eunice.
And they were brought up adopted.
And her sister had died in the meantime-- her twin sister.
She had nobody.
And she had been searching, researching for many, many years.
And she found her mother's maiden name, Bechhofer.
And I said, if you give me written permission that I may broadcast the name or-- or write about it, I might-- I'll try and help.
My sister got broadcast on Kol Israel radio.
I went to Israel during that year.
We broadcast the name with the result that Susi, who didn't know if she was Jewish-- she didn't know who she was-- she has a large orthodox family in New York.
The BBC made two films of her life.
It touched-- it had great repercussions, her life story.
And she is a completely different person.
I've felt rather guilty what I've done to her, because it turned her life upside down.
She played the church organ.
She was a committed Christian.
Now she's learning what it's like to be a Jew.
It's not an easy life for her.
But she-- and she's not the only one.
There's a most wonderful story, also somebody who phoned me up, who was a little bit older who knew that she was Jewish, Gwen Richards in Devon.
And she had been adopted by the Christian family.
And she said, still in my heart, I still feel like a Polish Jewess.
And she wrote me her story.
And for her, I found I had a-- by the time we became known as having a reunion, with a bookstore and everything, I had a letter from a Professor Schwartz in Jerusalem.
And he put a note in that his wife had written in, and she wrote the following-- I'm the only survivor of a very large family from Poland.
I was taken by the Germans with my large family into a concentration camp.
I'm the only survivor, she said.
If ever you come across the name Friedenreich, she said, please remember-- perhaps there, somewhere or other, is a cousin.
I know you've got letters going around-- letters going around all over the world.
And when Gwen Richards had written to me, she had sent me something that moved me to tears.
She was seven, and her sister, nine.
And they had kept their mother's letters for 50 years.
I didn't keep many letters, and those little children kept the letters.
And they couldn't read them anymore because they were written in German and in Yiddish.
I could read them.
And the name at the-- the letter was signed Friedenreich.
So all of a sudden, a cousin turns up in Israel.
And there are two-- you know, two people who think they're the only ones.
It is a most wonderful story of their meeting.
After the reunion, they came from Israel.
And several cousins met through this reunion.
And hundreds and hundreds of people are finding school friends, still to this day, relatives that they didn't even know existed.
And it happened because I asked for people's-- it happened because people started to write me letters.
They were such wonderful letters, I couldn't bear to destroy them.
They were life stories, histories.
So I thought, right.
I've got another great idea.
I'm full of great ideas, but I'm not very clever, you see.
I don't realize what it means to translate an idea into fact.
Anyway, I thought, I'll bring out a book.
Easy.
Very interesting stories.
I collected the letters, and I asked for more.
I got a lot of letters from all over the world.
And the only thing I specified, you have to state your name, former name, town of origin, and town of domicile now, above your story.
Otherwise I won't accept it.
To show it's a historical book and that we really existed.
And the letters came pouring in.
And also before them, the thing that really most reunited people and still reunites them today, is my wonderful brochure.
A friend of ours, late-- he became, also, my co-editor-- wonderful person, Shmuel Lowensohn.
He compiled this most wonderful, wonderful brochure.
And why is it so wonderful?
Because it contains the names, former names, towns of origin, and all the addresses of hundreds and hundreds of people, participants and nonparticipants at the reunion from all over the world.
And everybody went home with their brochure.
And other people who contributed-- it was very, very expensive to produce because of so much write-- written matter in it with tiny little printed names.
It went around the world.
It still goes around the world.
And the usual thing, when you have a brochure, you throw it out.
But this brochure, I don't think anybody ever threw one of those brochures away.
And people are writing to each other, and friends of friends of friends, they're getting to know each other.
You see, people don't understand-- if you have families, if you grow up in a normal way, you have lots of families-- cousins, and cousins, and aunts, and uncles, and second cousins.
You don't bother with all of them.
But if you're on your own in a str-- you know, came as a refugee, you haven't got any relatives, to find somebody who once knew your parents, to find an old school friend, it's a tremendous thing.
This is something that people do not realize.
And so this is what happened through this brochure, through the book, which is a story on it own.
It was very hard to produce.
I had to produce it myself in the end.
I couldn't get a-- I'm still cross with Weidenfeld.
Lord Weidenfeld could have spared the money for-- to produce a book which became as-- as famous as it is now, and it won a big prize, which I didn't put it in for.
But he thought it wasn't good selling matter.
It didn't earn enough money.
And so did all-- I went to all the Jewish big publishers.
Not only him.
And after three-- after three or four failures, I thought, no, I'm going to do it myself.
And not having any sort of-- I'm not being a rich person, but I had something that I've saved for 10 years and it just came due-- my life policy.
Two life policies, and they came to quite a lot of money.
I had been saving for 10 years.
And they just fell due in 1990.
And I took all that money and I went to what they call the vanity press, but the best of them.
That's the Book Guild.
They are very good, and they don't take on rubbish either.
So they put their all into this book.
They loved the book, and it's one of their biggest success stories because it's being reprinted all the time.
When I run short of books, I reprint.
And it really went around the world.
I had already made market from all the Kinder who contributed.
And it's-- it's really a work of history.
We didn't-- Shmuel and I didn't alter any of the stories, but we had to cut some down.
And that was very difficult, to keep the continuity.
But the brochure, more than this book even, results in ALL people getting to know each other all around the world.
We've got people-- and at the actual reunion, I mean, you can't imagine what it was like.
People describe it as a unique event.
They have never experienced anything like it.
IT ran for two days in the Harrow Leisure Center. 1,000 people from worldwide.
People just say, you were at the reunion?
That was-- you know, it's the people who were and the people who weren't.
It-- it was something really special.
The first day we sat at tables of origin.
Like, we had 20 tables from Vienna. 200 people from Vienna came. 200 people from Berlin.
Tables with Munich, with Frankfurt, with-- and the little-- people from little towns, they had several little town names.
It was absolutely-- I myself didn't really realize what was going on so much.
I was like the sort of the fairy on the-- on the Christmas tree, you know?
Or like the bride at the wedding.
You sit at the head table, you don't know what goes on.
But the feedback was tremendous.
We've got our own reunion film.
We've got our video.
The BBC made films.
The BBC made film, They made, They made-- the German crew sent-- the Germans sent a film crew.
Most wonderful film they made, called [SPEAKING GERMAN], as they were not allowed to be Germans anymore.
You know, they like long sentences.
And that was also a wonderful film.
One of the best I've ever seen.
And they were very well received by the crowd.
They were very surprised how well received they were by the Kinder.
Yeah.
Even the East Germans as [? were then ?] sent a little crew.
They also made a film.
And it was on the news in the morning.
It was on the news in the evening.
I became [LAUGHS] a celebrity overnight.
It was quite amazing.
I-- that's when I lost my inferiority complex because it became very, very big.
I feel quite-- you know, in a way, I don't call it my book.
It's our book.
It's our brochure.
It's our reunion.
Without the help, I couldn't have done it.
But the idea was mine, and it-- it really grew, and it's still growing.
We have another one in 194-- 1994, in Jerusalem.
That was sort of a 55th.
And please God, if I have the strength and energy, that will be probably the last one-- big one ever, the 60th.
I don't know.
It's-- it's three years away.
So we'll have our biggest diamond one, not in the year 2000, but in 1999, all being well.
But that will take some planning.
It'll probably take me two years to organize.
It's just a vague idea at the back of my mind.
But we've got groups meeting-- in America, they formed a group called KTA, Kindertransport Association.
Oh, they do everything in style.
They are very big.
They're much bigger than the mother office here in London.
I mean, everything is of the most ordinary material.
There's my friend Rita Rosenberg-- Rosenbaum, sorry.
Not Rosenberg.
Rita Rosenbaum.
She came to me about five years ago already.
We've been together in our office, just the two of us.
She's the clever one.
She does the computer.
I don't know how a computer works.
She's got all the names on the computer, all the subscriptions that come in once a year, and she does all that.
And we've been working together now for five years and the office is still running.
We pay our rent.
We've got a space in an office of one of the Kinder, in [?
Eric Weiss ?] travel agency in Curtain Road, and that's EC2.
And it takes me an hour to get to the office and then an hour back, and Rita the exactly the same time.
But we do it voluntary.
And It's a great-- you know, to be connected to all the other Kinder.
And they're all-- you know, when I get-- it is so unique that it overrides-- and I'm very proud of that fact-- it overrides religion, education.
It overrides financial, and it's-- it's one of the most unique groups in the world because everybody's called by their first name.
We've got a Nobel Prize winner.
We've got another one with something very similar to a Nobel Prize.
But he's the same friend to me as somebody who's, unfortunately, in hospital.
We've got people in mental homes who never came to terms with what happened after-- after peace broke out.
And they are part of out group.
And if I find somebody in Switzerland-- that was only about three years ago.
I had two members in Switzerland.
I put them in touch with one another.
Now we've got four members in Switzerland.
And they've become a group.
They've become such close friends you can't believe it.
It's-- it's quite unique.
Thank you very much.
Yes.
Now, they are different.
They're a different group again.
In fact, I won't say we've had trouble.
But we feel-- all of us feel-- a guilt complex, as opposed to them.
They have their meetings in the Holocaust Survivors Center.
Now, I wonder if they call us Holocaust survivors?
We are survivors, of course, but we haven't gone through the holocaust.
We-- [INAUDIBLE] we're quite friendly together but we do not mix.
Nor with the hidden children, because it would be too big.
We cannot encompass every-- anything so vast.
I mean, there are-- there were 10,000-- nearly 10,000 of us, I have found worldwide.
I would think we have found-- and a lot of us have died in the last few years-- we have found about three and a half thousand, which is a lot after 50 years.
And they are worldwide.
But we are too big to amalgamate with the hidden children.
I put the idea into their heads that they should also write a book of reminiscences because it's very, very important.
And-- and the survivors, the Holocaust survivors, are a group on their own.
We are the Kindertransportees.
And we, although we're very friendly and we-- we talk-- I recommend them as speakers, and they recommend me as speakers.
I spend my life now speaking to a lot-- last year I was in-- in South Africa sponsored by the-- the equivalent of the JIA in South Africa.
And I go a lot to Germany.
At first it was very painful.
I've been now four times.
And they receive me with open arms.
And the new Germans, I must say-- the ones who want to know, the schools I've spoken to-- They are more interested than the schools in England.
I don't get enough invitations from schools in England.
In fact, I've never had an invitation from an English school.
You see, I haven't got a PR.
What you call in England, I haven't got anybody who gives me the publicity to go out and talk.
Lots of us go out and talk.
Lots of us.
In Sheffield, in Scotland, I've got, though, it seems she leads a wonderful group of Kinder and other survivors.
She has mixed hers.
And a lot of us go out and speak to schools, but it's mostly Jewish schools.
But in Germany, I speak to the German schools.
I do groups there-- Christian churches, Jewish groups, mixed-- there are a lot of Counsel of Christian Jews there.
I find their interest more than the Jewish interest, and that really amazes me.
I would very much like to get an invitation from, say, [INAUDIBLE] College.
I've never had one.
Emmanuel College-- never had one.
I had to ask the JFS if I can give a talk there.
I had to ask the-- what is the-- the orthodox grammar school?
Hasmonean?
Hasmonean.
I asked them, yes, and I gave a talk there.
But the feedback is not as great as I get.
I suppose Germany's got more interest.
It's-- they've got more reason to want to know.
And we're sheltering our children also.
They-- you know how Jewish people like to shelter their children.
But in America, I mean, there's a big network of speakers.
But I've never been asked, officially, here in England, from any education department.
Which is a pity in a way because now-- and with the pictures I've got.
I take my picture along with me, and they're huge.
They are by a meter by a meter.
The archive-- if you wanted to photograph one or two, they are terrific.
In fact, they were all around the wall of the Harrow Leisure Center.
Paula Hill, when she researched-- and Stephanie Colasanti is a wonderful photographer.
We've got a great lot of talent among the Kinder.
We reproduced those pictures, and they were all around the wall in the Harrow Leisure Center.
And people made a beeline for them to recognize themselves on-- on the wall.
And that was a tremendous thing.
It's-- you know, it's-- really, I can't believe I've started it.
It's because I'm not clever, you see?
A clever person would think of the consequences, what it entails.
But if you're not-- like my teacher said, you'll never amount to anything.
So, well-- so-- But you're proving her wrong.
So I've proved her wrong.
I wish she were alive to tell her.
That's what happened.
It's-- it's become quite big.
And everybody wants to be a Kind now, which is quite unique.
My own children?
Well, it's only recently-- now they're very proud of me.
But they never asked me questions before.
You know, it was something we hid.
We didn't like--
Being ?] refugees because we didn't have such a wonderful reception here.
In England, in the wartime, it wasn't good because our English wasn't perfect enough.
You were a so-and-so German.
You were a Nazi.
You see, they didn't-- couldn't understand.
The ordinary English people couldn't understand the difference between a German and a German Jew.
It was-- you know, it's-- I can understand it.
The-- the educated ones perhaps knew.
But the help that was given by many people, it must be stressed-- nearly 60 years ago when we came-- by non-Jewish people, churches, Jewish people, individuals.
Tremendous.
I could-- you know, it-- it would take hours and hours to tell you the individual stories that-- that happened.
But I think you have to read my book.
I don't know if you've got it.
Advertising my book.
But it is a-- it's a special one.
And it should be all over in America.
I-- I went to a reunion in America, 1990, soon-- soon after the book was published.
And in the whole of America, I've only sold 500 books.
And there's still some left, which is quite-- really appalling, in a way.
Yes.
It's a new career.
But also, you know, I do the selling, and-- but I think people ought to know about it.
It's not because it's mine.
It's not my story.
It's the stories of the people.
And Shmuel worked very hard with me.
Unfortunately, he died about three years ago.
It's terrible how many people we've lost.
I-- I don't know what to put it down to.
Another thing I don't know what to put down to is the education, the tremendous high education many of us-- myself excluded, I'm sorry to say-- have achieved.
Tremendous.
I mean, people who were not-- we were brought here with the purpose of not being educated.
They told us, don't expect an education here.
You are not here to be educated.
You're here to do, probably, the menial jobs and do the jobs the ordinary people don't want to do.
But how the people got educations, I don't know.
They did it themselves.
Children-- little children without any encouragement from-- from any sources got wonderful education.
I've got a friend who-- who never was satisfied because she never had an education.
I wish I could do it one day.
She insisted she got a BA now.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After having gone through cancer, she insists she has got a BA.
So, you know, it's something in you.
And you probably want to prove that you've got something in you.
You want to stress the positive side.
But the fact that so many people lost their parents.
I always say we 20% who found our parents again-- approximately 15% to 20%-- are tremendously lucky and feel guilty.
Most of us do.
And to come to terms, for so many children, never have to found their parents.
Never have grandparents.
Their children never had grandparents.
Terrible.
When they got married, had children, they hadn't got a mother to turn to, to help with the baby.
It's a dreadful thing.
And a lot of the people married each other, especially in the hostels.
We've got a lot of happy hostel weddings, and we've got a lot of 50th anniversaries coming up just now, the older ones.
But it must be stressed that we have many young ones.
The youngest was four months-- the one I knew personally was four months.
Lots of babies of 18 months.
There's a wonderful story in the book of two twins being pushed in-- little babies in a-- in a basket being pushed in the train, and how the children looked after them.
So, there's some incredible stories.
The thing is now I've sort of woken it's all up again.
And there's still some people who don't want to know.
They don't want to be reminded.
I can't fault that, you know?
I can understand it.
But as we're old and retiring, this is the time.
Somebody said, why didn't you do it 25 years ago?
It wasn't the right time.
I didn't think of it.
We had children to bring up.
We had weddings to make.
We had money to earn, a living to earn.
We didn't have time for things like that.
We weren't ready for it.
But now we're retired.
That's when also a lot of the rot sets in.
A lot of us now need mental support coming to terms with all the things that happened so long ago.
But I'm still glad I've sort of preserved it.
And just the thought, 49 years after the event that nobody was doing anything about it, gave me the idea.
Otherwise, it would have been completely forgotten.
So that's my work now.
Thank you.
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
And thank the cameraman, the crew, and the ladies behind the scenes.
It's a lot of work you're doing.
And thank Mr.
Spielberg, because without him, this project would have never got off the ground.
I know lots of projects exist.
[NON-ENGLISH] lots of things exist.
But he's doing a tremendous project.
Thank God he can afford it.
Yeah.
